name: analysis.ipynb — experiment results methodology
applicability: Complete framework for analyzing autonomous agent experiment history: load TSV, compute keep/discard/crash rates, plot progress with running minimum, rank improvements by delta. Reusable pattern for any agent that runs iterative experiments (skill-audit scores, deep-research quality, GSD phase verification).
home_connection: Apply to agent performance tracking. Your review-metrics.jsonl and learning-routes.jsonl could use similar progress visualization.

name: karpathy/nanochat (parent repo)
applicability: Full nanochat implementation with wider platform support. Flash Attention fallbacks, generic device support. The code patterns are ML-specific, not transferable to your Node/TS stack.
home_connection: None direct. Conceptual reference for how autoresearch simplified from a larger codebase.

name: miolini/autoresearch-macos (Mac fork)
applicability: Community adaptation of autoresearch for Mac/MPS. Interesting as a case study of how the 3-file architecture enables platform forking — the fork only needed to modify train.py.
home_connection: Validates the 3-file architecture: immutable/editable/instructions enables clean forks.

name: andyluo7/autoresearch (multi-GPU fork)
applicability: Multi-GPU extension. The scaling pattern (single → multi GPU) is conceptually interesting for multi-agent scaling but code is ML-specific.

name: TinyStories dataset (HuggingFace)
applicability: GPT-4 generated short stories for smaller-compute experiments. Not relevant to SoNash but validates the fixed-budget pattern works at different scales.

name: .gitignore reveals multi-agent architecture
applicability: worktrees/ (parallel experiments), queue/ (work distribution), CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md (per-session generation). The public repo is single-agent; multi-agent infrastructure exists but is gitignored. This hidden architecture is the most interesting unexplored aspect of the repo — it's where autoresearch goes beyond 'one agent, one loop'.
home_connection: Multi-agent experiment patterns. Your worktree usage for parallel work is similar in principle.
